definition: Service-Level Objective — a target for a reliability metric (e.g. 99.9% of requests succeed under 300ms), the internal goal an SLA promises externally.
Service-Level Agreement — the contractual availability/performance commitment to customers, with penalties if breached.
Dead-Letter Queue — a side queue that captures messages a consumer fails to process after N retries, so poison messages don't block the main queue and can be inspected later.
Origin Access Control — the modern way to lock an S3 origin so it is reachable only through CloudFront, not directly over the internet (replaces the legacy OAI).
Origin Access Identity — the legacy CloudFront identity used to restrict direct S3 access; superseded by OAC.
Point-In-Time Recovery — continuous backups that let you restore a datastore (DynamoDB, RDS, Aurora) to any second within the retention window.
Web Application Firewall — AWS WAF filters HTTP(S) traffic at the edge with managed and rate-based rules to block common exploits and abuse before they reach the origin.
Key Management Service — AWS's managed service for creating and controlling encryption keys; the basis for encryption at rest with auditable rotation.
Server-Side Encryption — the service encrypts data at rest on write and decrypts on read (e.g. S3 SSE-KMS, SSE-S3).
Identity and Access Management — AWS's permissions system; least-privilege scoped roles replace long-lived credentials.
Virtual Private Cloud — your isolated private network in AWS; private subnets in a VPC keep data tiers off the public internet.
Network Address Translation gateway — lets resources in a private subnet reach the internet for egress (e.g. pulling updates) without being publicly reachable; billed hourly plus per-GB processed.
Application Load Balancer — a Layer-7 load balancer that routes HTTP(S) traffic to targets; billed per hour plus per LCU.
Load Balancer Capacity Unit — the metered usage unit (new connections, active connections, bandwidth, rule evaluations) that drives ALB/NLB cost.
Simple Queue Service — a managed message queue for decoupling producers from consumers and absorbing bursts; at-least-once delivery, so consumers must be idempotent.
Simple Notification Service — managed pub/sub for fan-out and notifications (email, SMS, HTTP, Lambda); commonly the target of a CloudWatch alarm.
A serverless event bus for routing events between AWS services and apps with content-based rules; used for fan-out and event-driven decoupling.
Running across multiple Availability Zones (AZs — isolated data centers in a region) so a single-AZ failure doesn't take the system down; the core reliability lever between tiers.
AWS X-Ray — distributed tracing that follows a request across service boundaries to find latency and errors.
Records AWS API/management activity for audit and incident investigation — distinct from operational application logs.
Capture metadata about IP traffic to and from network interfaces in a VPC, for security analysis and audit.
Relational Database Service — managed relational databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, etc.); supports Multi-AZ and read replicas.
Amazon's cloud-native relational engine (MySQL/PostgreSQL-compatible) with a distributed storage layer, fast replicas, and Multi-AZ failover.
A fully managed NoSQL key-value/document database; on-demand capacity absorbs spiky load without capacity planning.
Serverless compute for containers (ECS/EKS) — run containers without managing EC2 instances; billed per vCPU- and GB-hour.
Elastic Container Service — AWS's container orchestrator; runs tasks on Fargate or EC2.
Managed user sign-up/sign-in and identity (user pools + identity pools); billed per monthly active user.
Managed in-memory cache (Redis/Memcached) in a VPC private subnet; reduces datastore load and latency.
Managed search and analytics (the Elasticsearch fork); runs in a VPC for full-text search and log analytics.
The window over which point-in-time recovery can restore a datastore.
A consumer that produces the same result if it processes the same message more than once (e.g. dedupe on an idempotency key) — required because queues deliver at-least-once.
Pre-warmed Lambda execution environments that eliminate cold-start latency for a steady or spiky baseline, at an always-on cost.
A read-only copy of a database that offloads read traffic and can be promoted on failover, improving availability and read scalability.
Network traffic between Availability Zones (e.g. Multi-AZ replication) — billed per GB and a real cost of high availability.
